DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-24-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: DEFENDANT SFBSC MANAGEMENT, LLC'S DEMURRER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT.

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Rya M. Nelson, Esq., of Bracamontes & Vlasak, P.C., (415) 835-6777, for Plaintiffs.
Tammara N. Bokmuller, Esq., of Nelson Mullins Riley & Scarborough LLP, (619) 489-6110, for Defendant SFBSC Management, LLC.

The matter is ARGUED. At the conclusion of argument, the court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendant SFBSC Management, LLC's demurrer to the second amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to the first through sixth, tenth and nineteenth causes of action and overruled as to the eight, eleventh, twenty-third and twenty-fourth causes of action. Plaintiffs' FEHA claims are time-barred because plaintiffs did not file an administrative complaint within three years of the conduct which they contend violates the FEHA and their 2024 administratie complaint does not relate back to their earlier filed administrative complaint. Plaintiff's PAGA claim is time-barred because their PAGA notice was submitted more than a year after the alleged PAGA violations and their 2024 PAGA does not relate back to their earlier submitted PAGA notice. Plaintiffs' conversion, wrongful termination, and battery claims are not time-barred because SFBC was brought into this case by Doe amendment on March 20, 2022 and these claims relate back to the filing of the initial complaint because they are based on the same general facts as alleged in the initial complaint.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling, and in compliance with CRC 3.1312, and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org.

DEFENDANT SFBSC MANAGEMENT, LLC'S Motion To Stay Action.

Defendant SFBSC Management, LLC's motion to stay this action pending resolution of the Bijou Century, LLC Chapter 11 bankruptcy case is granted. Off Calendar Jun-27-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Michael Bracamontes, Esq. (415.835.6777; mbracamontes@bclawsf.com) of Bracamontes & Vlasak PC for Plaintiffs.
Tammara Bokmuller, Esq. (619.489.6110; Tammy.Bokmuller@nelsonmullins.com) of Nelson Mullins Riley & Scarborough LLP for Defendant, SFBSC Management, LLC.
Shane Cahill, Esq. (415.397.2222; scahill@longlevit.com) of Long & Levit LLP for Defendants, Bijou Century, LLC et al.

ARGUED The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SFBSC MANAGEMENT, LLC'S MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OR STAY OR DISMISS.

Defendant SFBSC Management, LLC's motion to quash service of summons is denied.

SFBSC moves under CCP 474, asserting that plaintiffs' Doe amendment naming it was unreasonable delayed. The CCP 474 "phrase 'ignorant of the name of a defendant' is broadly interpreted to mean not only ignorant of the defendant's identity, but also ignorant of the facts giving rise to a cause of action against that defendant." (Fuller v. Tucker (2000) 84 Cal.App.4th 1163, 1170.) Moreover, "section 474 is to be liberally construed" and the "fact that the plaintiff had means to obtain information is irrelevant." (Id.)

Here, I credit plaintiffs' statement that the Marlin deposition first gave them knowledge "that there was zero administrative staff at NCT [New Century Theater] and that SFBSC was essentially running the entire back office operation." This is key in a case largely based on wage and hour claims.

Prejudice to SFBSC is not undue. Plaintiffs' first Doe application was filed in March 2022, the suit in late 2020. Though, as NCT's "back office," SFBSC would seem to already have most of the relevant documents, it may seek further discovery when NCT emerges from its Chapter 11 bankruptcy.

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

The judge pro tem's report and recommendation is adopted. Plaintiffs in this private attorney general act case were nude dancers at defendant's club. Discovery of contact information for other dancers at the club shall be permitted. However, privacy interests in this occupation are heightened. Thus, that discovery shall be via an opt-out procedure operated by a third-party administrator using her or his usual process and paid for by defendant. The parties shall meet and confer regarding selection of an administrator. If they cannot agree, further selection proceedings shall be conducted upon a motion filed in Department 301.
